summary: Continuation of the chatzi shi'ur dispute and its implications for oaths. The Gemara explores Rabbi Akiva's view that oaths can prohibit any amount, what excludes a gambler from testimony, and the full chatzi shi'ur dispute with proofs from a baraita about the koy.

display.whats: Oath on Yom Kippur eating: Rabbi Akiva's view and the gambler exclusion
display.hint: Can an oath create a new eating prohibition even when Torah permits a half-measure?
display.title: R. Akiva and the Half-Measure Oath Problem

learning.ahaMoment: Rabbi Akiva's position allows oaths to apply even to half-measures, solving the oath-conflict problem Reish Lakish faced.
learning.memoryAnchor: A gambler's exclusion from testimony appears here as a legal footnote - the verse requires someone who can make declarations.
learning.learnerQuestion: How does R. Akiva's position on oaths resolve the question of whether an oath can validly apply to a half-measure of Yom Kippur food?
learning.coreTension: A half-measure of Yom Kippur food is not punishable by karet. Can an oath even apply to eating such an amount? And what does the gambler's exclusion from testimony have to do with this discussion?
learning.coreMove: R. Akiva holds that oaths can restrict even Torah-permitted amounts. This means an oath not to eat any Yom Kippur food is valid even for sub-karet amounts like the half-measure. The gambler's exclusion from testimony appears as a legal data point in the surrounding analysis about oaths and their scope.

takeaway.text: R. Akiva's broad view of oaths extends them to Torah-permitted amounts, making the half-measure oath valid. Without this position, an oath not to eat any Yom Kippur food would have no operative effect for the sub-karet amount.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume oaths can only apply to halachically significant amounts (those carrying penalties). R. Akiva extends oaths to amounts below the karet threshold, giving oaths broader reach than the underlying prohibition.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Akiva: oaths apply to any amount
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Akiva
argumentFlow.text: Even what Torah permits can be self-forbidden by oath

argumentFlow.label: Oath may not override Torah permit
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If Torah says permitted, can oath re-prohibit?

argumentFlow.label: Gambler excluded from testimony
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Acha bar Yaakov
argumentFlow.text: The oath-formula verse excludes those unfit to testify

argumentFlow.label: Gambler: not fit for declarations
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'If he does not tell' - gambler is excluded

halachic: Rabbi Akiva: oaths can restrict amounts Torah permits
gamblers unfit as witnesses for oaths

quizSeeds.question: How does Rabbi Akiva's view resolve the oath problem with chatzi shi'ur?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabbi Akiva holds oaths can restrict even Torah-permitted amounts, so an oath not to eat any Yom Kippur food is valid even for half-measures

sugyot.finalRuling: Rabbi Akiva resolves the oath problem: oaths can apply to any amount.

display.whats: Full chatzi shi'ur dispute with proof from baraita and koy
display.hint: What evidence does each side bring for whether chatzi shi'ur is forbidden by Torah or rabbinically?
display.title: Chatzi Shi'ur: Biblical or Rabbinic? The Core Dispute

learning.ahaMoment: Two separate questions are easy to blur into one: whether the mishna's 'forbidden' covers the half-measure at all, and whether that coverage is Torah-level or only rabbinic. The mishna's own wording settles only the first.
learning.memoryAnchor: Two questions, not one: does 'forbidden' reach the half-measure (yes), and is that reach biblical or rabbinic (still disputed).
learning.learnerQuestion: Is eating a half-measure (chatzi shi'ur) of Yom Kippur food prohibited by Torah law or only rabbinically, and what is the basis for each position?
learning.coreTension: Karet applies only to a full measure. What is the status of a half-measure - is there a Torah-level prohibition, or only rabbinic protection? And does the Mishnah's word 'forbidden' resolve this?
learning.coreMove: R. Yochanan holds chatzi shi'ur is a Torah prohibition because the Torah forbids 'any' eating. Reish Lakish holds it is only rabbinic since there is no karet for it. The Mishnah's word 'forbidden' adds content beyond the karet verse - covering the half-measure - but does not resolve the level (biblical vs. rabbinic) of the prohibition.

takeaway.text: The chatzi shi'ur dispute is foundational: R. Yochanan makes the half-measure biblically prohibited; Reish Lakish makes it rabbinic. The Mishnah's 'forbidden' establishes scope (half-measures are included) but leaves the level to be argued by the amoraim.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may conflate the scope question (does 'forbidden' cover half-measures?) with the level question (is the prohibition biblical or rabbinic?). These are two separate questions, and the Mishnah only answers the first.

argumentFlow.label: Karet always implies forbidden
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: But here mishna needs to teach the half-measure case

halachic: chatzi shi'ur: Torah (Rabbi Yochanan) vs rabbinic (Reish Lakish)

quizSeeds.question: What does the mishna's use of 'forbidden' add over the word 'karet' in the context of Yom Kippur eating?
quizSeeds.answer: It teaches that even a chatzi shi'ur (half-measure) is forbidden, even though it is not punishable by karet

sugyot.finalRuling: Chatzi shi'ur: Rabbi Yochanan holds Torah prohibition; Reish Lakish holds rabbinic. The mishna's 'forbidden' teaches the half-measure case.

display.whats: Full chatzi shi'ur dispute with proof from baraita and koy
display.hint: What evidence does each side bring for whether chatzi shi'ur is forbidden by Torah or rabbinically?
display.title: Beyond Karet: What 'Forbidden' Adds to the Yom Kippur Eating Prohibition

learning.ahaMoment: Rabbi Yochanan does not just assert his position, he brings a baraita that limits liability language to the full measure, which only makes sense if the half-measure is still forbidden without karet. The koy citation then pulls the evidence back toward a rabbinic reading.
learning.memoryAnchor: A baraita about liability and a citation about the koy: two pieces of evidence pulling in opposite directions on whether chatzi shi'ur is Torah-level or rabbinic.
learning.learnerQuestion: What additional content does the Mishnah's word 'forbidden' contribute to the Yom Kippur eating prohibition, beyond the already-stated karet penalty?
learning.coreTension: The Torah imposes karet for eating on Yom Kippur. The Mishnah also uses the word 'forbidden.' If karet is already known, what independent content does 'forbidden' add?
learning.coreMove: The Mishnah's 'forbidden' extends the prohibition to amounts below the karet threshold - teaching that even a chatzi shi'ur (half-measure) is forbidden even though it carries no karet. The word creates a distinct prohibition layer: full-measure eating triggers karet; sub-karet amounts are separately 'forbidden.'

takeaway.text: The Mishnah's 'forbidden' adds an independent prohibition layer below karet. This creates a two-tier structure: karet for full-measure willful eating, a separate prohibition status for the half-measure.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may read 'forbidden' as simply restating the karet prohibition in milder terms. The Gemara shows it carries independent legal content - covering amounts that the karet verse alone would not reach.

argumentFlow.label: Chatzi shi'ur dispute stated
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yochanan: Torah; Reish Lakish: rabbinic

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Yochanan's baraita proof
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: A baraita limits liability to the full measure - implies half is not Torah-exempt but is still prohibited

argumentFlow.label: Koy suggests rabbinic phrasing
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The baraita's koy case suggests the category is rabbinic

halachic: chatzi shi'ur: Torah (Rabbi Yochanan) vs rabbinic (Reish Lakish)

quizSeeds.question: What does the mishna's use of 'forbidden' add over the word 'karet' in the context of Yom Kippur eating?
quizSeeds.answer: It teaches that even a chatzi shi'ur (half-measure) is forbidden, even though it is not punishable by karet

sugyot.finalRuling: Chatzi shi'ur: Rabbi Yochanan holds Torah prohibition; Reish Lakish holds rabbinic. The mishna's 'forbidden' teaches the half-measure case.

glossary.en: An animal of uncertain classification (neither clearly domesticated nor wild), whose halakhic status is doubtful in several areas.
A gambler; classified as unfit to testify in court under rabbinic law.
Liable for the karet penalty - the Talmud distinguishes between 'forbidden' (less than the measure) and 'liable for karet' (the full measure).

rashiTranslations.he: ואליבא דר' עקיבא דאמר. במס' שבועות (דף יט:) אדם הנשבע שלא
rashiTranslations.en: and according to Rabbi Akiva who says a person can forbid himself anything in any amount through an oath

rashiTranslations.he: אוכל סתם אוסר עצמו בכל שהוא דהכי תנן שבועה שלא אוכל ואכל
rashiTranslations.en: if you say: since half-measure has Torah permission, the oath-prohibition cannot take effect on it

rashiTranslations.he: כל שהוא ר' עקיבא מחייב וחכמים פוטרים הלכך הא דקתני חייב אי
rashiTranslations.en: Rav Acha bar Yaakov: 'any judge' excludes a gambler - a gambler is unfit to testify

rashiTranslations.he: כרבנן סבירא ליה מוקמינן ליה במפרש ואי כרבי עקיבא סבירא
rashiTranslations.en: it is different there because the verse says 'if he does not tell' - a gambler is unfit to make declarations

rashiTranslations.he: ליה מוקמינן ליה אפי' בסתם ורבי
rashiTranslations.en: anywhere the mishnah says 'punishable by karet' it also implies 'forbidden' - but here the opposite was needed

rashiTranslations.he: שמעון פוטר ר' שמעון לטעמיה
rashiTranslations.en: or say: when the mishna says 'forbidden' it refers to the additional items (the baraita list beyond karet)

rashiTranslations.he: דאמר בכל שהוא נמי מושבע מסיני
rashiTranslations.en: the chatzi shi'ur dispute itself: Rabbi Yochanan says forbidden by Torah; Reish Lakish says rabbinic

rashiTranslations.he: הוא דתניא במסכת מכות (דף יז.)
rashiTranslations.en: Rabbi Yochanan challenges Reish Lakish: a baraita says 'I only have one who ate the full amount' - implying the half is not included

rashiTranslations.he: ר' שמעון אומר כל שהוא למכות ולא
rashiTranslations.en: so too, it is reasonable to say this is rabbinic - a koy (uncertain species) is cited regarding blood

rashiTranslations.he: אמרו כזית אלא לענין קרבן ואי
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 10]: אמרו כזית אלא לענין קרבן ואי

rashiTranslations.he: סבירא ליה לריש לקיש דחצי שיעור
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 11]: סבירא ליה לריש לקיש דחצי שיעור

rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 12]: אסור מדרבנן היכי מוקי לה להא

rashiTranslations.he: דתני חייב באוכל חצי שיעור והרי אף
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 13]: דתני חייב באוכל חצי שיעור והרי אף

rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 15]: (דברים יז) : וכי תימא כיון דאית ליה
